nativeRange.habitat: Found on seaward rocky and coral reef slopes, where juveniles tend to inhabit deeper, coral-rich areas while adults are commonly seen on shallower, exposed reef faces.

decorPreferences: well-established live rock with mature film algae
open swimming lanes along the front of the tank

diet.notes: The Chevron Tang is an obligate algae grazer that browses film and detrital algae from rockwork; its bristletooth dentition is built for sweeping fine algal mats rather than scraping hard substrate. Daily offerings of dried marine algae (nori) clipped to the glass, along with algae- and spirulina-enriched frozen and pellet foods, help meet its nutritional needs. Long fasting periods are not well tolerated.

goodWith: most reef fish outside the tang family
clownfish and damsels of similar or larger size
shrimp, snails and other reef invertebrates
avoidWith: highly aggressive surgeonfishes that may bully a newly-introduced specimen

summary: The Chevron Tang is a Hawaiian bristletooth famous for its dramatic juvenile coloration: fiery orange with bold dark chevron markings that mature into a uniform dark olive adult body with fine green pinstriping.
careNotes: Among the gentler members of the Ctenochaetus genus toward unrelated tankmates, but still tang-territorial toward other surgeonfishes and intolerant of conspecifics outside very large systems. Generally advised to be the only bristletooth in the tank. Has been reported to do best in mature reef aquariums (8+ months old) with abundant film and turf algae for constant grazing; supplement with daily dried marine algae and algae-enriched frozen foods to maintain coloration and resistance to head-and-lateral-line erosion (HLLE), which tangs are prone to under poor nutrition or chronically poor water quality. Adults shed the bright juvenile chevron pattern and become uniformly dark with fine longitudinal green lines — buyers expecting the juvenile look should be aware this transformation is permanent. As with all Acanthuridae, the species carries a sharp caudal-peduncle scalpel that can lacerate handlers and tankmates, so net carefully and use a specimen container when moving. Reef-safe in most cases, though older adults have occasionally been observed nipping at SPS coral while grazing.
breedingNotes: Has not been bred in home aquaria. Like other Acanthuridae, Ctenochaetus hawaiiensis is a broadcast spawner releasing pelagic eggs that develop through an extended planktonic acronurus larval stage — captive rearing requirements are not well documented and are considered impractical for hobbyists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade source for husbandry: tank size 180 gal, semi-aggressive, reef safe, herbivore, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, care level moderate, adult ~11 in.

additional.name: reefapp.net encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, range, diet, and reef-safety. Lists 400 L minimum (lower than LiveAquaria's 681 L); averaged with cautious upward bias to 700 L for adult size of ~28 cm.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), range (Pacific Ocean Oceania), max length 25 cm TL, depth 0-61 m, wild temperature 24.9-29 C, film-algae feeder.

additional.name: Wikipedia: Chevron tang
additional.notes: Confirms 25 cm max, juvenile orange-red with dark chevrons vs adult dark with fine green stripes, range Ryukyus to Hawaii and French Polynesia, fifth most-exported aquarium fish from Hawaii.

fish.breedingNotes: The Chevron Tang is a broadcast spawner with an extended planktonic acronurus larval stage, and no home-aquarium breeding success has been documented.
